title: Axiom corpus citation-path grammar
description: Versioned, machine-readable grammar for the `citation_path` field of every record in data/corpus/provisions/**/*.jsonl. A citation_path is the stable public identity of a corpus provision: the provision UUID is uuid5(NAMESPACE_URL, 'axiom:' + citation_path) (see schema/citation-path.v1.md and src/axiom_corpus/ingest/supabase.py). Any change to a path changes the provision's identity. This document is derived empirically from the committed corpus (see the `derivation` block) and is enforced by scripts/validate_citation_paths.py. It is additive and descriptive: it does not rename any existing path. The regexes here are the normative check; the prose companion (citation-path.v1.md) is explanatory.

derivation.note: Counts are a point-in-time snapshot used to document coverage. They are not asserted by the validator except through the ratcheted known-irregulars totals below; the validator recomputes live counts on each run.

citation_path.description: Slash-delimited hierarchical identifier. Segment 0 is the jurisdiction; segment 1 is the document_class; segments 2+ are the source-specific hierarchy down to a leaf provision. No leading slash, no trailing slash, no empty segments. A bare '<jurisdiction>/<document_class>' path (no hierarchy segment) is valid only for collection-root records (kind='collection', level=0).
citation_path.$comment: The pattern encodes: <jurisdiction>/<document_class>(/<segment>)*. Each hierarchy segment starts with an alphanumeric and may contain letters, digits, spaces, dots, hyphens, and en-dashes (U+2013). The zero-hierarchy case matches collection roots. Segments that END in a hyphen/space (truncated source-heading slugs) match the pattern but are tracked and capped via known_irregulars_ratchet.truncated_segments so they cannot proliferate. See per-part definitions below for semantics.

jurisdiction.description: Segment 0. A national jurisdiction slug ('us','uk','be') or a national slug with a subnational suffix ('us-ca','be-vlg'). Must equal the record's `jurisdiction` field. The observed universe below is the closed set of slugs currently in the corpus; the pattern is intentionally slightly broader so new states/regions validate without a schema bump, but new slugs SHOULD be added to `observed` in a follow-up.

conventions.national: Bare two/three-letter ISO-ish code: 'us', 'uk', 'be'. Federal / national-level source text lives here (e.g. Title 26 IRC under 'us', UK primary legislation under 'uk', Belgian federal 'loi' under 'be').
conventions.subnational: national '-' region: US states 'us-XX' (postal, lowercase); Belgian regions 'be-vlg' (Flanders), 'be-wal' (Wallonia), 'be-bru' (Brussels), 'be-dg' (German-speaking Community).

document_class.description: Segment 1. A closed enumeration. Must equal the record's `document_class` field.

semantics.statute: Codified primary legislation (US Code titles, UK ukpga, Belgian loi/decreet/ordonnance, state codes).
semantics.regulation: Administrative / secondary legislation (CFR, state administrative codes, UK uksi, Belgian arrete).
semantics.manual: Agency operational manuals / handbooks / eligibility source books (state SNAP/TANF manuals). Largest class by volume; frequently paginated (see page-N).
semantics.guidance: Sub-regulatory guidance (IRS revenue procedures, SSA automatic determinations, agency ACLs/ACINs).
semantics.policy: State plans and policy documents (TANF state plans, SNAP E&T state plans).
semantics.form: Official forms and their published parameter tables (tax forms, CMS eligibility-level tables).
semantics.rulemaking: In-progress rulemaking documents (e.g. NJAC proposed-rule dockets).

hierarchy_segment.description: Segments 2+. Source-specific hierarchy tokens. Character set is [A-Za-z0-9], space, dot '.', hyphen '-', en-dash U+2013. Must start with an alphanumeric. Uppercase, spaces, and en-dashes are permitted because they occur in real published citation labels (see irregular_families). Well-formed segments end in an alphanumeric or dot; segments ending in a hyphen or space are truncated-slug artifacts, tracked under known_irregulars_ratchet.truncated_segments.

families.description: The recurring path shapes below are the segment-2+ conventions per (jurisdiction, document_class) cluster. They are documentary: the validator does not require a path to match one of these, only that every segment matches `hierarchy_segment`. New families are expected as coverage grows.

us_federal_statute.notes: USLM-derived. <title> is the numeric US Code title (26 IRC, 42 Social Security Act host title). Subsection depth mirrors USLM (a, 1, A, i...). Aspirational target form 'us/statute/26/32/a/1' (EITC) follows this family once Title 26 §32 is ingested.

us_federal_regulation.notes: eCFR-derived. <title> numeric CFR title (7, 42, 45, 47). Note 'subpart-A' style segments. IMPORTANT: rulespec-us module paths for the same law use a DIFFERENT namespace ('us/regulations/7-cfr/273/9'); the corpus form is the canonical citation_path and is what module `source_verification.corpus_citation_path` fields must point at.

uk_statute.notes: legislation.gov.uk short form. Section tokens carry letter suffixes (1K, 228ZA, 783A).

belgium_statute.notes: ELI-style. <instrument> is the language/type token ('loi','arrete','decreet','decret','ordonnance'). Date is split into yyyy/mm/dd segments. <eli-id> is the numeric Belgian identifier. Article tokens include 'N' (annex) and may carry bis/ter/quater suffixes and spaces in some ingests. A parallel 'moniteur'/'justel' variant family (be/statute/moniteur/<date>/... and be/statute/justel/<eli>/...) appears in Belgian ingests beyond r0; it obeys the same segment charset and is a known-irregular family, not a violation.

us_state_manual_paginated.notes: PDF/HTML manuals split into ordinal pages. See irregular_families.page_n.

us_state_regulation.notes: State administrative code. Note that where a section is not further segmented, its content is carried in a single 'block-1' leaf (see irregular_families.block_n). This is the crux of axiom-programs#14: program specs sometimes reference a deeper subsection ('.../365/180/A') than the corpus segments.

irregular_families.description: Segment idioms that deviate from a clean legal-hierarchy tokenization but are VALID and expected. Each carries a live count (recomputed by the validator) and a ratcheted maximum-additions budget in known_irregulars_ratchet. They exist because the source document does not expose a finer structure at that point.

block_n.meaning: A synthetic leaf holding an un-subdivided chunk of source text (a whole section, a table, or a paragraph run) where the extractor could not or did not derive a legal sub-identifier. Predominantly state manuals and state regulations.

page_n.meaning: An ordinal page of a paginated source (PDF or long HTML). Structural, not legal: page-12 is 'page 12 of this document', not 'part 12'.

uppercase_segments.meaning: Segments containing uppercase letters: US Code subsection letters (subpart-A), UK section suffixes (228ZA), Belgian 'N' annex articles, and verbatim state-code labels (NH 'He-W 766.01').

space_segments.meaning: Segments containing internal spaces, carried verbatim from published citation labels. Concentrated in Alaska ('7 AAC 45.010') and New Hampshire ('He-W 766.01').

endash_segments.meaning: Segments containing an en-dash (U+2013), from ranged labels in source text.

truncated_segments.meaning: A hierarchy segment that ends in a hyphen or a space because the source heading was truncated when the slug was built (e.g. '...confidential-information-by-'). These are a data-quality smell, not a legal idiom: the trailing punctuation carries no meaning and the slug is incomplete. Entirely within us-ut/manual/dws/eligibility-manual at r0. Tracked and capped so re-ingests do not add more; the durable fix is upstream slug generation.

collection_roots.meaning: A bare '<jurisdiction>/<document_class>' path with no hierarchy segment. These are collection container records (kind='collection', level=0), not leaf provisions. Valid but structurally distinct: they have no parent and are not groundable targets.

known_irregulars_ratchet.description: A ratcheted ceiling on irregular-family membership, checked by scripts/validate_citation_paths.py. The validator FAILS if a live count exceeds its baseline (a regression: someone introduced more irregular segments than existed at r0) unless the baseline is deliberately raised in this file. It does NOT fail if a count drops (irregulars being cleaned up). Baselines were measured against origin/main @ 9ee50de. Raising a baseline is a reviewed decision recorded in this file's git history.

identity_drift_ratchet.description: Paths whose stored provision `id` matches NEITHER the path-only identity uuid5(NAMESPACE_URL,'axiom:'+citation_path) NOR the versioned identity uuid5(NAMESPACE_URL, json.dumps(['axiom', version, citation_path])). A non-empty set means a path was edited AFTER its UUID was minted, so the stored identity no longer derives from the current path — exactly the failure mode this grammar exists to prevent. These are tracked, not tolerated: the validator fails if the set GROWS beyond the baseline list, and the fix is to re-key (re-ingest) the provision and emit the path-mapping events specified in schema/citation-path.v1.md so downstream (rulespec `corpus_citation_path`, claims, reverse index) can follow. See docs/granularity-policy-proposal.md; several of these are the corpus side of axiom-programs#14.

identity.consequence: citation_path IS provision identity. Changing a path is a rename/split/merge event, not an edit. See schema/citation-path.v1.md for the required path-mapping event vocabulary.
